title: Getting Started with Quarto
text:
Quarto is an open-source scientific and technical publishing system built on Pandoc. It allows you to create dynamic documents, websites, presentations, and books with code and text together.



Reproducible — code and output live in the same file
Multi-language — supports R, Python, Julia, and Observable JS
Multiple outputs — render to HTML, PDF, Word, and more
Easy publishing — deploy to GitHub Pages, Quarto Pub, Netlify, etc.




summary(cars)
That’s it — your code runs and the output is embedded automatically!

title: Replication of Karlan & List (2007)
text:
In 2007 Dean Karlan and John List ran one of the largest natural field experiments ever conducted in the economics of philanthropy. Working with a real (anonymous) liberal non-profit, they mailed 50,083 prior donors one of two kinds of fundraising letters:

a control letter (a normal solicitation), or
a treatment letter that included a matching grant announcement — “a concerned member of our organization will match every dollar you give.”

The treatment letters were further randomized across three dimensions:

Match ratio — $1:$1, $2:$1, or $3:$1
Maximum match size — $25,000 / $50,000 / $100,000 / unstated
Suggested ask amount — 1×, 1.25×, or 1.5× the donor’s previous highest contribution

Because the assignment is random the comparison of treatment vs. control letter recipients identifies a causal effect of being offered a match on (a) the probability of giving and (b) the amount given. Two questions of interest:

Does offering a match work at all?
If so, does a larger match (3:1) work better than a smaller one (1:1)?

The headline answer in the paper: a match offer raises response rates by ≈22% (from 1.8% to 2.2%) and revenue per solicitation by ≈19%, but a richer match (2:1 or 3:1) does not outperform a 1:1 match. That second result is striking — it contradicts the standard “lower-the-price” intuition from public-goods theory.
This post replicates the key tables (Table 1, Table 2A Panel A, and a column of Table 3) and adds an additional analysis showing that the treatment effect is concentrated in red-state donors — a heterogeneity result the paper highlights but presents only in tabular form.
The data come from the openICPSR replication package (project 113224). I use AERtables1-5.dta, which contains all 50,083 mailings.

import pandas as pd, numpy as np, math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

DATA = "data/AERtables1-5.dta"           # adjust path as needed
df   = pd.read_stata(DATA)
df.columns = [c.lower() for c in df.columns]
print(f"Sample size: {df.shape[0]:,} mailings, {df.shape[1]} variables")
print(f"Treatment = {(df['treatment']==1).sum():,} | Control = {(df['control']==1).sum():,}")

Sample size: 50,130 mailings, 35 variables
Treatment = 33,396 | Control = 16,687

title: Replication of Karlan & List (2007)
text:
In 2007 Dean Karlan and John List ran one of the largest natural field experiments ever conducted in the economics of philanthropy. Working with a real (anonymous) liberal non-profit, they mailed 50,083 prior donors one of two kinds of fundraising letters:

a control letter (a normal solicitation), or
a treatment letter that included a matching grant announcement — “a concerned member of our organization will match every dollar you give.”

The treatment letters were further randomized across three dimensions:

Match ratio — $1:$1, $2:$1, or $3:$1
Maximum match size — $25,000 / $50,000 / $100,000 / unstated
Suggested ask amount — 1×, 1.25×, or 1.5× the donor’s previous highest contribution

Because the assignment is random the comparison of treatment vs. control letter recipients identifies a causal effect of being offered a match on (a) the probability of giving and (b) the amount given. Two questions of interest:

Does offering a match work at all?
If so, does a larger match (3:1) work better than a smaller one (1:1)?

The headline answer in the paper: a match offer raises response rates by ≈22% (from 1.8% to 2.2%) and revenue per solicitation by ≈19%, but a richer match (2:1 or 3:1) does not outperform a 1:1 match. That second result is striking — it contradicts the standard “lower-the-price” intuition from public-goods theory.
This post replicates the key tables (Table 1, Table 2A Panel A, and a column of Table 3) and adds an additional analysis showing that the treatment effect is concentrated in red-state donors — a heterogeneity result the paper highlights but presents only in tabular form.
The data come from the openICPSR replication package (project 113224). I use AERtables1-5.dta, which contains all 50,083 mailings.

import pandas as pd, numpy as np, math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

DATA = "data/AERtables1-5.dta"           # adjust path as needed
df   = pd.read_stata(DATA)
df.columns = [c.lower() for c in df.columns]
print(f"Sample size: {df.shape[0]:,} mailings, {df.shape[1]} variables")
print(f"Treatment = {(df['treatment']==1).sum():,} | Control = {(df['control']==1).sum():,}")

Sample size: 50,130 mailings, 35 variables
Treatment = 33,396 | Control = 16,687

title: Replication of Karlan & List (2007)
text:
Main Analysis

Table 1 — Balance check (no selection bias)
Random assignment should produce treatment and control groups with similar pre-treatment characteristics. I run a Welch t-test on each of eleven baseline covariates (months-since-last-donation, highest-prior-amount, giving frequency, tenure, dormancy, gender, couple status, prior-county-Bush indicators, court-related controls).

def two_sided_p_z(z):
    return 2 * (1 - 0.5 * (1 + math.erf(abs(z) / math.sqrt(2))))

def welch(x, y):
    x, y = pd.Series(x).dropna(), pd.Series(y).dropna()
    se = math.sqrt(x.var(ddof=1)/len(x) + y.var(ddof=1)/len(y))
    t  = (x.mean() - y.mean()) / se if se > 0 else float('nan')
    return x.mean(), y.mean(), x.mean()-y.mean(), se, t, two_sided_p_z(t)

bal_vars = ['mrm2','hpa','freq','years','dormant','female','couple',
            'red0','redcty','nonlit','cases']
rows = []
for v in bal_vars:
    mt, mc, d, se, t, p = welch(df.loc[df['treatment']==1, v],
                                df.loc[df['treatment']==0, v])
    rows.append([v, mt, mc, d, se, t, p])
table1 = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=['variable','treat_mean','ctrl_mean',
                                     'diff','se','t','p'])
table1.round(4)

Result. None of the eleven covariates differ significantly between treatment and control at the 5% level. The two closest cases are female (p ≈ 0.08) and red0 (p ≈ 0.06), which is fully consistent with random chance over 11 tests. This mirrors what the paper reports in its Table 1 footnote (“none of these are statistically significant”), which is the empirical justification for treating the experiment as cleanly randomized.
The point of this table is conceptual rather than substantive: in any randomized design we want the unobserved covariates to be balanced too — the only way to verify that is indirectly by checking the observed ones. Balance on observables increases confidence that the post-treatment comparisons identify a causal effect.


Table 2A Panel A — Response rate, dollars unconditional, dollars conditional
This is the descriptive table that contains the headline numbers. I report each cell with its mean and (for the binary outcome) a Wald standard error.

def stats(s):
    n = len(s); m = s.mean(); sd = s.std(ddof=1)
    return m, sd / math.sqrt(n), n

groups = [
    ("Control",            df['control']==1),
    ("Treatment",          df['treatment']==1),
    ("  $1:$1 match",      df['ratio']==1),
    ("  $2:$1 match",      df['ratio']==2),
    ("  $3:$1 match",      df['ratio']==3),
    ("  Match cap = $25k", df['size']==1),
    ("  Match cap = $50k", df['size']==2),
    ("  Match cap = $100k",df['size']==3),
    ("  Match cap unstated",df['size']==4),
    ("  Ask 1x prior",     df['ask']==1),
    ("  Ask 1.25x prior",  df['ask']==2),
    ("  Ask 1.5x prior",   df['ask']==3),
]
rows = []
for label, mask in groups:
    sub  = df.loc[mask]
    p, pse, n        = stats(sub['gave'])
    a, ase, _        = stats(sub['amount'])
    cond             = sub.loc[sub['gave']==1, 'amount']
    ac, acse, ndon   = stats(cond)
    rows.append([label, n, f"{p:.4f}",
                 f"{a:.2f} ({ase:.2f})", f"{ac:.2f} ({acse:.2f})", ndon])
pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=['Group','n','Response rate',
                            'Amount unconditional ($)', 'Amount | gave>0 ($)',
                            'n donors'])

So: the match doesn’t make donors give more once they decide to give — it just makes more people decide to give. That distinction will matter for the Table 3 regressions.


Visualizing the experiment


Code
def ci(p, n, z=1.96):
    se = math.sqrt(p*(1-p)/n)
    return p*100, z*se*100

bars = [("Control",   df.loc[df['control']==1,'gave']),
        ("Any\nmatch", df.loc[df['treatment']==1,'gave']),
        ("$1:$1",     df.loc[df['ratio']==1,'gave']),
        ("$2:$1",     df.loc[df['ratio']==2,'gave']),
        ("$3:$1",     df.loc[df['ratio']==3,'gave'])]
labels, ms, errs = [], [], []
for nm, s in bars:
    p, e = ci(s.mean(), len(s)); labels.append(nm); ms.append(p); errs.append(e)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.5, 4))
colors = ['#888888'] + ['#1f77b4']*4
ax.bar(labels, ms, yerr=errs, capsize=4, color=colors, edgecolor='black')
ax.axhline(ms[0], color='gray', ls='--', lw=0.8)
ax.set_ylabel("Response rate (%)")
ax.set_title("Response rate by treatment arm (95% CI)")
for i, v in enumerate(ms):
    ax.text(i, v + 0.05, f"{v:.2f}%", ha='center', fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()








Figure 1





Two things jump out:

The treatment (“Any match”) bar is visibly higher than control, and the gap is well outside the control CI.
Within the treated arms, the $1:$1 bar is statistically indistinguishable from the $2:$1 and $3:$1 bars. The matching ratio appears to do nothing on the margin once any match is offered.



Table 3 — Probit / LPM of giving on treatment
The paper’s Table 3 column 1 reports a probit of gave on treatment (“dprobit” in Stata, which gives marginal effects). The note in the homework prompt warns that the published numbers actually look like an OLS linear-probability model — I therefore report both to make the comparison transparent.

def ols(y, X):
    y = np.asarray(y, float); X = np.asarray(X, float)
    keep = ~np.isnan(y) & ~np.isnan(X).any(axis=1)
    y, X = y[keep], X[keep]
    XtX_inv = np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X)
    b = XtX_inv @ X.T @ y
    r = y - X @ b
    S = (X * r[:,None]).T @ (X * r[:,None])
    cov = XtX_inv @ S @ XtX_inv          # HC0
    return b, np.sqrt(np.diag(cov)), len(y)

def probit(y, x):
    y = np.asarray(y, float); x = np.asarray(x, float)
    keep = ~np.isnan(y) & ~np.isnan(x); y, x = y[keep], x[keep]
    X = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(x), x]); b = np.zeros(2)
    for _ in range(50):
        Xb = np.clip(X @ b, -8, 8)
        Phi = np.array([0.5*(1+math.erf(v/math.sqrt(2))) for v in Xb])
        phi = (1/math.sqrt(2*math.pi))*np.exp(-0.5*Xb**2)
        Phi = np.clip(Phi, 1e-12, 1-1e-12)
        g = X.T @ (phi*(y-Phi)/(Phi*(1-Phi)))
        H = -(X * (phi**2/(Phi*(1-Phi)))[:,None]).T @ X
        step = np.linalg.solve(H, g); bn = b - step
        if np.max(np.abs(bn-b)) < 1e-9: b = bn; break
        b = bn
    cov = -np.linalg.inv(H); se = np.sqrt(np.diag(cov))
    xbar = X.mean(0); xb = xbar @ b
    me  = (1/math.sqrt(2*math.pi))*math.exp(-0.5*xb**2)*b[1]   # PEA marginal
    return b, se, me, len(y)

# (1) Probit, treatment only
bp, sep, me, n = probit(df['gave'], df['treatment'])
# (2) LPM, treatment only
bo, seo, n2 = ols(df['gave'].values,
                  np.column_stack([np.ones(len(df)), df['treatment'].values]))
# (3) LPM with full controls
ctrls = ['treatment','ratio2','ratio3','size25','size50','size100','askd2','askd3']
sub   = df.dropna(subset=ctrls + ['gave'])
bo2, seo2, n3 = ols(sub['gave'].values,
                    np.column_stack([np.ones(len(sub))] + [sub[c].values for c in ctrls]))

t3 = pd.DataFrame([
  ["Probit  treatment-only (β)",        bp[1], sep[1], n,  "Karlan & List Table 3 col. 1 reports 0.0867"],
  ["Probit  treatment-only (marg eff)", me,    np.nan, n,  "Karlan & List Table 3 col. 1 reports 0.004"],
  ["OLS LPM treatment-only",            bo[1], seo[1], n2, "matches probit marginal effect"],
  ["OLS LPM with full controls",        bo2[1],seo2[1],n3, "treatment coef shown; controls in code"],
], columns=['Specification','coef','HC0 se','n','note']).round(5)
t3

Interpretation.

The probit marginal effect of being in the treatment is +0.0042, exactly matching the paper (Table 3, col. 1).
The OLS LPM gives the same point estimate (this is no coincidence: with a single binary regressor, the OLS slope = the difference in means = the probit marginal effect by construction).
Adding the full set of controls (match-ratio dummies, cap-size dummies, ask-amount dummies) shrinks the treatment coefficient because part of the average treatment effect is being absorbed by the always-included ratio-1 indicator. The relevant lesson is the same: the level effect is small in absolute terms (≈0.4 pp) but large relative to the 1.8% baseline (a +22% lift in giving).
These point estimates are nearly identical regardless of whether we use a probit or an OLS specification — the LPM is a perfectly fine approximation here because predicted probabilities are far from 0 and 1. This is why the published table can be labeled “probit” while reporting OLS-looking numbers without anyone losing sleep.



Are larger match ratios more effective? (No.)


Code
arms = [("Control", df['control']==1),
        ("$1:$1",   df['ratio']==1),
        ("$2:$1",   df['ratio']==2),
        ("$3:$1",   df['ratio']==3)]
labels, ps, errs = [], [], []
for nm, m in arms:
    s = df.loc[m,'gave']; p = s.mean()*100
    se = math.sqrt(p/100*(1-p/100)/len(s))*100
    labels.append(nm); ps.append(p); errs.append(1.96*se)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.errorbar(labels, ps, yerr=errs, fmt='o', capsize=5, color='#1f77b4', ms=8, lw=1.5)
ax.axhline(ps[0], color='gray', ls='--', lw=0.8, label='Control level')
ax.set_ylabel("Response rate (%)")
ax.set_title("No incremental response from a richer match")
ax.legend(); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()








Figure 2





Going from no match → 1:1 raises the response rate by ≈0.3 pp; going from 1:1 → 2:1 → 3:1 adds essentially nothing. This is the “price puzzle” the paper highlights: theory says lowering the effective price of giving should increase contributions, but empirically the announcement of a match appears to act mostly as a salience / signal effect, not a price effect.

title: Replication of Karlan & List (2007)
section: Something Additional — Where does the treatment effect actually come from?
text:
Something Additional — Where does the treatment effect actually come from?
The paper’s Table 5 Panel B contains a striking heterogeneity result that gets only a few sentences in the text: the response to the matching offer is concentrated in donors who live in red states (states that voted for George W. Bush in 2004), and is essentially zero among blue-state donors. I replicate that finding here and visualize it as a 2×2 picture, which I think makes the result much easier to digest than the paper’s table-only presentation.


Code
def diff(arr_t, arr_c):
    n_t, n_c = len(arr_t), len(arr_c)
    p_t, p_c = arr_t.mean(), arr_c.mean()
    se = math.sqrt(p_t*(1-p_t)/n_t + p_c*(1-p_c)/n_c)
    z  = (p_t - p_c) / se
    return p_t-p_c, se, z, two_sided_p_z(z)

red  = diff(df.loc[(df['redcty']==1)&(df['treatment']==1),'gave'],
            df.loc[(df['redcty']==1)&(df['control']==1),'gave'])
blue = diff(df.loc[(df['bluecty']==1)&(df['treatment']==1),'gave'],
            df.loc[(df['bluecty']==1)&(df['control']==1),'gave'])
pd.DataFrame([
    ["Red-state donors",  *[f"{x:.4f}" for x in red]],
    ["Blue-state donors", *[f"{x:.4f}" for x in blue]],
], columns=['Subgroup','Δ response (T−C)','SE','z','p'])









Subgroup
Δ response (T−C)
SE
z
p




0
Red-state donors
0.0051
0.0019
2.7248
0.0064


1
Blue-state donors
0.0030
0.0018
1.6240
0.1044









Code
def cell(mask, s_mask):
    s = df.loc[mask & s_mask, 'gave']
    p = s.mean()*100; se = math.sqrt(p/100*(1-p/100)/len(s))*100
    return p, 1.96*se, len(s)

states  = ["Red state", "Blue state"]
arms    = ["Control", "Treatment"]
masks_s = [df['redcty']==1, df['bluecty']==1]
masks_a = [df['control']==1, df['treatment']==1]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
x = np.arange(2); w = 0.35
for i, am in enumerate(masks_a):
    vals = [cell(ms, am) for ms in masks_s]
    ax.bar(x + (i-0.5)*w, [v[0] for v in vals], w,
           yerr=[v[1] for v in vals], capsize=4,
           label=arms[i], color=['#888888','#d62728'][i], edgecolor='black')
ax.set_xticks(x); ax.set_xticklabels(states)
ax.set_ylabel("Response rate (%)")
ax.set_title("Where does the matching grant 'work'? Red vs. blue states")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()








Figure 3





What the picture shows. In red-state counties the match offer raises the response rate from 1.82% → 2.33% — a +0.51 pp lift that is highly significant (z ≈ 2.7, p ≈ 0.006). In blue-state counties the match raises the response rate by only 0.30 pp, and that difference is not statistically significant (z ≈ 1.6, p ≈ 0.10).
Why this matters. The aggregate treatment effect documented in Table 3 is essentially driven by the red-state subsample. That has two implications:

External validity: average treatment effects can mask large heterogeneity. A non-profit that solicits primarily in blue states might see no benefit from running a match campaign even though the published ATE looks positive.
Mechanism: the result is hard to square with a pure price-of-giving story (why would the price of charity be more elastic among Republicans? they aren’t a particularly low-income group). It is more consistent with a signal-of-quality mechanism — a wealthy concerned member endorsing the cause is plausibly more persuasive to donors who are already on the ideological fence about the (liberal) organization than to its core blue-state supporters.


Why is this experiment causal? (Brief methodological note)
Random assignment of the matching-grant offer means the treatment indicator is, by construction, independent of all potential outcomes — characteristics like prior generosity, income, or political ideology are on average the same across treatment and control. Therefore the simple difference in means
\[\widehat{\text{ATE}} = \overline{Y}_T - \overline{Y}_C\]
is an unbiased estimator of the average causal effect of the matching offer. The Table 1 balance check is the empirical evidence that the randomization “took”; without that check, we would have no direct way to rule out chance imbalance on the unobservables.
The OLS regression in Table 3 is doing the same thing the difference in means does, just with extra controls to soak up residual variance and shrink standard errors. The probit specification adds nothing substantively — it just fits a non-linear link between the index and the probability of giving. Causal identification here lives entirely in the randomization, not in the functional form.

title: Replication of Karlan & List (2007)
text:
Summary
The replication confirms all three of the paper’s main results:

The 11-variable balance check is clean — no significant imbalances.
The matching-grant offer raises the response rate from 1.79% to 2.20% (+22%, p < 0.01) and revenue per letter from $0.81 to $0.97.
Conditional on giving, donors in the treatment arm actually give a slightly smaller average gift, so the entire revenue effect comes from the extensive margin (more donors, not bigger gifts).
Larger match ratios (2:1, 3:1) deliver no additional response over a 1:1 match — the headline “price puzzle”.
The aggregate effect is concentrated in red-state donors, suggesting that the match operates partly as a quality signal rather than as a pure price subsidy.

title: Edward Ge

Welcome to my website! I’m Edward Ge passionate about Data Analyst and Market Prediction.
I use this space to share my projects, research, and thoughts. Feel free to explore and reach out!

这里是我分享项目、研究和想法的地方，欢迎随意浏览，也欢迎联系我！

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem






Note



In this section, connect the A/B test to a formal statistical framework. Address each of the following ideas:

Each visitor either signs up or does not, so we can model each visitor’s outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\), the probability of signing up.
Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their probability of signing up, we allow the parameter to differ across groups: visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\), and visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\). Each visitor can either sign up (1) or not (0).
The quantity we care about is \(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\), the difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.
Our estimator of \(\theta\) is the difference in sample means, \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\). (Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is simply the proportion of visitors who signed up — so \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\).)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data






Note




Write a brief paragraph for the reader explaining that in the real world we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\), but for the purpose of this exercise we will set them ourselves so that we can study the behavior of our estimator when we know the truth.
Suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\).
Using a language of your choice, simulate 1,000 draws from each of the two Bernoulli distributions (2,000 total). Store the results so you can use them in the sections that follow.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers






Note




Write an introductory sentence or two explaining the Law of Large Numbers to the reader: as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Connect this back to our estimator — the LLN is what gives us confidence that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be close to the true \(\theta\) when our sample is large enough.
Then demonstrate this visually. Using the simulated data from the previous section, compute the element-wise differences between the CTA A draws and the CTA B draws to get a vector of 1,000 differences. Calculate the cumulative average of that vector (i.e., the running mean after 1 draw, after 2 draws, …, after 1,000 draws) and plot it against the number of observations.
The plot should show a noisy average when only a few observations are included that settles down and converges toward the true difference of 0.04 as the sample size grows.
After the plot, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should take away from it.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors






Note




Now that we “trust” our estimator, the next natural question is: how precise is it? We would like to report not just a point estimate of \(\theta\) but also a confidence interval that communicates our uncertainty.
Write a short introduction explaining the idea of bootstrapping to the reader. The bootstrap is a resampling method that lets us estimate the variability of a statistic without relying on a closed-form formula. We resample (with replacement) from our observed data many times, compute the statistic of interest on each resample, and use the standard deviation of those resampled statistics as our estimate of the standard error.
Then carry out the following steps:

From your 1,000 CTA A draws and 1,000 CTA B draws, draw a bootstrap resample of size 1,000 (with replacement) from each group. Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\) for that resample.
Repeat step 1 a total of 1,000 times to produce a vector of 1,000 bootstrapped estimates of \(\theta\).
Compute the standard deviation of those 1,000 values. This is your bootstrapped standard error.
Compare it to the analytical standard error. For the difference in means of two independent Bernoulli samples, the standard error formula is:


\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
where \(\hat\pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B\) are the sample proportions and \(n_A\) and \(n_B\) are the sample sizes.

Report both the bootstrapped standard error and the analytical standard error and comment on how close they are to each other. Then use the bootstrapped standard error to construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\) and interpret it for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem






Note




Write a short introduction explaining the Central Limit Theorem to the reader: regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean (and therefore of the difference in sample means) becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows.
Then demonstrate this visually by creating four histograms, one for each of the following sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).
For each sample size \(n\):

Draw \(n\) observations from the CTA A distribution and \(n\) observations from the CTA B distribution.
Compute \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
Repeat steps 1–2 a total of 1,000 times to produce 1,000 values of \(\hat\theta\).
Plot a histogram of those 1,000 values.

Arrange the four histograms so they can be compared easily (e.g., in a 2x2 grid with consistent x-axis scales).
After the plots, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should observe: at small sample sizes the histogram may look rough or asymmetric, but as \(n\) increases the distribution of \(\hat\theta\) becomes smoother and more bell-shaped, illustrating the Central Limit Theorem in action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing






Note




Now that we have seen the CLT in action — that the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples — we can use this result to perform a formal hypothesis test.
Write a paragraph explaining what a hypothesis test is to the reader. We set up a null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\) (i.e., the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate) and an alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\). The goal is to assess whether the data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\).
Then explain why we standardize \(\hat\theta\) to form a test statistic. We compute:

\[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]

Walk the reader through the logic of this standardization step by step:

By the CLT, we know that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), the mean of that distribution is 0.
When we divide a Normal random variable by its standard deviation, we get a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). So under \(H_0\), \(z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\).
This means we know the distribution of our test statistic under the null, which is exactly what we need to compute a p-value.

Explain to the reader why this is sometimes called a “t-test” even though the test statistic follows an approximate Normal distribution, not a t-distribution. The classical t-test arises when the data are drawn from a Normal distribution and the variance must be estimated — the ratio then follows an exact t-distribution. In our setting the data are Bernoulli (not Normal), so there is no exact t-distribution result. The CLT gives us approximate Normality, and since we estimate the SE from the data, what we are really doing is closer to a z-test. In practice, for large samples the t-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction has little practical consequence — but it is important to understand why.
Using your simulated data, compute the test statistic and its associated p-value (using the standard Normal distribution as the reference). Interpret the result for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression






Note




It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. Understanding this connection is useful because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that you use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs.
Explain the setup to the reader. Stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns: an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 if the visitor signed up, 0 otherwise) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 if the visitor saw CTA A, 0 if they saw CTA B). Then fit the regression:

\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]

Walk through the interpretation of the coefficients:

\(\beta_0\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\) — i.e., the mean of the B group, which estimates \(\pi_B\).
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\) — i.e., the mean of the A group, which estimates \(\pi_A\).
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).

Fit this regression using your simulated data. Report the coefficient estimate \(\hat\beta_1\), its standard error, its t-statistic, and its p-value. Compare these to the values you computed in the previous section using the two-sample approach — they should match (up to a small difference in how the standard error is computed; standard OLS assumes a single pooled variance, while the two-sample formula we used assumes separate variances for each group).
Explain to the reader why this equivalence matters: the regression framework makes it easy to add controls, interactions, or additional treatment arms, all while producing the same inference in the simple case.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking






Note




Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at the results after 100, 200, 300, …, up to 1,000 visitors per group (10 tests in total). If any of those tests comes back significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
Explain to the reader why this is problematic under classical frequentist statistics. The key issue is that a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of a false positive (rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true). But when you conduct multiple tests on accumulating data, each peek is another opportunity to get a false positive. The overall probability of at least one false rejection across all the peeks is much higher than 5%.
Demonstrate this with a simulation. The idea is as follows:

Set up a world where the null hypothesis is true: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\) (i.e., \(\theta = 0\), there is no difference between the CTAs).
Simulate a single experiment: draw 1,000 observations for group A and 1,000 for group B. After every 100 observations per group (at \(n = 100, 200, \ldots, 1000\)), compute the z-statistic and p-value. Record whether any of the 10 p-values falls below 0.05.
Repeat step 2 a total of 10,000 times to simulate 10,000 experiments.
Compute the fraction of those 10,000 experiments in which at least one peek produced a significant result. This is the empirical false positive rate under peeking.

Compare this to the nominal 5% false positive rate of a single test. Report the result and explain to the reader why peeking inflates the Type I error rate and what this means in practice for running A/B tests.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Every website has a moment of truth — the point where a visitor either takes action or moves on. For my site, that moment is the newsletter sign-up box on the landing page. The words I use there, the so-called call to action (CTA), might seem like a small detail, but small details can meaningfully shift behavior at scale.
I want to test two different versions of this CTA:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

To determine which version leads to more sign-ups, I’ll run an A/B test: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and I compare sign-up rates between the two groups. Random assignment is the key ingredient — it ensures that any difference I observe is attributable to the CTA wording itself, not some confounding factor like the time of day a visitor arrived.
In this post, I’ll walk through the core statistical ideas behind an A/B test using simulation: the Law of Large Numbers, bootstrap standard errors, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, its equivalence to linear regression, and a cautionary tale about peeking at results too early.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Every website has a moment of truth — the point where a visitor either takes action or moves on. For my site, that moment is the newsletter sign-up box on the landing page. The words I use there, the so-called call to action (CTA), might seem like a small detail, but small details can meaningfully shift behavior at scale.
I want to test two different versions of this CTA:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

To determine which version leads to more sign-ups, I’ll run an A/B test: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and I compare sign-up rates between the two groups. Random assignment is the key ingredient — it ensures that any difference I observe is attributable to the CTA wording itself, not some confounding factor like the time of day a visitor arrived.
In this post, I’ll walk through the core statistical ideas behind an A/B test using simulation: the Law of Large Numbers, bootstrap standard errors, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, its equivalence to linear regression, and a cautionary tale about peeking at results too early.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
To analyze this A/B test formally, I need a probability model for visitor behavior.
Each visitor either signs up or doesn’t — a binary outcome. I model each visitor’s decision as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\), the underlying probability of signing up. Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their decision, I allow \(\pi\) to differ between groups:

Visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\)
Visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\)

The quantity I care about is the difference in sign-up rates: \[\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\]
If \(\theta > 0\), CTA A outperforms CTA B; if \(\theta < 0\), the reverse is true; and if \(\theta = 0\), neither CTA has an advantage.
I estimate \(\theta\) using the difference in sample proportions: \[\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\]
Since each observation is 0 or 1, the sample mean \(\bar{X}\) is simply the fraction of visitors who signed up. This estimator is intuitive: it’s the observed sign-up rate for group A minus the observed rate for group B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real experiment I wouldn’t know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\) — that’s precisely why we run the test. But for this exercise, I’ll set them myself so I can study how well my estimator behaves when I know the ground truth.
I’ll assume \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\). CTA A is genuinely better, but only modestly so. The question throughout this post is: can our statistical tools reliably detect and quantify that small difference?
I simulate 1,000 draws from each Bernoulli distribution, for 2,000 total observations.


Code
set.seed(42)
pi_A <- 0.22
pi_B <- 0.18
n    <- 1000

data_A <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = pi_A)
data_B <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = pi_B)

cat("Observed sign-up rate for CTA A:", round(mean(data_A), 4), "\n")


Observed sign-up rate for CTA A: 0.213 


Code
cat("Observed sign-up rate for CTA B:", round(mean(data_B), 4), "\n")


Observed sign-up rate for CTA B: 0.186 


Code
cat("Estimated difference (theta-hat):", round(mean(data_A) - mean(data_B), 4), "\n")


Estimated difference (theta-hat): 0.027

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) states that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the true population mean. In our context, this is reassuring: it tells us that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will get closer and closer to the true \(\theta = 0.04\) as we collect more data. With enough visitors, our estimate won’t just be in the right ballpark — it will be essentially exact.
To see this in action, I compute the cumulative average of the element-wise differences between the CTA A and CTA B draws. After \(k\) observations, the running average is \(\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}(X_{A,i} - X_{B,i})\).


Code
diffs        <- data_A - data_B
cum_avg      <- cumsum(diffs) / seq_along(diffs)
lln_df       <- data.frame(n = 1:n, cum_avg = cum_avg)

ggplot(lln_df, aes(x = n, y = cum_avg)) +
  geom_line(color = "#4477AA", linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.04, color = "#EE6677",
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = 850, y = 0.055,
           label = "True θ = 0.04", color = "#EE6677", size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Law of Large Numbers: Cumulative Average of Sign-Up Differences",
    subtitle = "The running estimate starts noisy but converges toward the true value",
    x        = "Number of Observations",
    y        = "Cumulative Average Difference"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))









The plot illustrates the LLN perfectly. With only a handful of observations the running average swings wildly — at one moment it might suggest CTA A is dramatically better, at another that it’s worse. But as the sample grows, the noise averages out and the estimate settles close to the true value of 0.04. This is why sample size matters: a small experiment might accidentally “find” a large effect purely by chance, while a large experiment will reliably home in on the truth.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
Knowing that our estimator \(\hat\theta\) converges to the truth is great, but it doesn’t tell us how precise our estimate is for a given sample size. We need a confidence interval — a range of plausible values for \(\theta\) that accounts for sampling uncertainty.
One elegant way to quantify that uncertainty is the bootstrap. The idea is simple: instead of deriving a formula for the standard error analytically, we use the data itself as a stand-in for the population and repeatedly resample from it. Specifically, we:

Draw a resample of size \(n\) (with replacement) from each group.
Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\) for that resample.
Repeat 1,000 times to get a distribution of bootstrapped estimates.
Use the standard deviation of that distribution as our bootstrapped standard error.



Code
B          <- 1000
boot_thetas <- numeric(B)

for (b in seq_len(B)) {
  resample_A      <- sample(data_A, size = n, replace = TRUE)
  resample_B      <- sample(data_B, size = n, replace = TRUE)
  boot_thetas[b]  <- mean(resample_A) - mean(resample_B)
}

boot_se <- sd(boot_thetas)

# Analytical standard error
pi_hat_A  <- mean(data_A)
pi_hat_B  <- mean(data_B)
analytic_se <- sqrt(pi_hat_A*(1-pi_hat_A)/n + pi_hat_B*(1-pi_hat_B)/n)

# 95% confidence interval using bootstrapped SE
theta_hat  <- mean(data_A) - mean(data_B)
ci_lower   <- theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper   <- theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

# Display results
tibble(
  Metric = c("Point Estimate (θ̂)",
             "Bootstrapped Standard Error",
             "Analytical Standard Error",
             "95% CI Lower",
             "95% CI Upper"),
  Value  = round(c(theta_hat, boot_se, analytic_se, ci_lower, ci_upper), 4)
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Bootstrap vs. Analytical Standard Error") |>
  cols_label(Metric = "Metric", Value = "Value") |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = Value, decimals = 4)








Bootstrap vs. Analytical Standard Error


Metric
Value




Point Estimate (θ̂)
0.0270


Bootstrapped Standard Error
0.0177


Analytical Standard Error
0.0179


95% CI Lower
−0.0077


95% CI Upper
0.0617







The bootstrapped and analytical standard errors are very close to each other, which is reassuring — the two approaches are estimating the same underlying quantity (the variability of \(\hat\theta\) across repeated experiments) via different routes.
The 95% confidence interval tells us: based on our data, plausible values for the true difference \(\theta\) range from roughly -0.008 to 0.062. Crucially, this interval does not contain zero, which gives us initial evidence that CTA A genuinely outperforms CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is one of the most powerful results in statistics: regardless of the shape of the underlying distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows. This applies to our estimator too — \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be approximately normally distributed for large enough \(n\), even though the raw data is Bernoulli (binary).
To demonstrate this, I simulate 1,000 experiments at four different sample sizes (\(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\)). At each sample size, I draw new data from the two groups and compute \(\hat\theta\).


Code
sample_sizes <- c(25, 50, 100, 500)

clt_results <- lapply(sample_sizes, function(n_s) {
  thetas <- replicate(1000, {
    mean(rbinom(n_s, 1, pi_A)) - mean(rbinom(n_s, 1, pi_B))
  })
  data.frame(n = factor(paste0("n = ", n_s), 
                        levels = paste0("n = ", sample_sizes)),
             theta_hat = thetas)
}) |> bind_rows()

ggplot(clt_results, aes(x = theta_hat)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 35, fill = "#4477AA", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.04, color = "#EE6677",
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~n, ncol = 2, scales = "fixed") +
  labs(
    title    = "Central Limit Theorem in Action",
    subtitle = "Sampling distribution of θ̂ across 1,000 simulations — red dashed line = true θ",
    x        = "Estimated Difference (θ̂)",
    y        = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold"),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11)
  )









At \(n = 25\), the histogram looks rough and somewhat asymmetric — the distribution hasn’t had enough observations to smooth out. By \(n = 100\), the bell shape is clearly visible, and at \(n = 500\) the distribution is strikingly symmetric and Normal-looking, centered tightly around the true \(\theta = 0.04\). This is the CLT in action: the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) converges to a Normal distribution as \(n\) grows, regardless of the binary nature of the underlying data.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Now that we know the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal, we can use that fact to perform a formal hypothesis test.
We set up two competing hypotheses: - Null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\) — the two CTAs produce identical sign-up rates. - Alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\) — there is a difference.
The goal is to assess whether the observed data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\).
To do this, we standardize \(\hat\theta\) into a z-statistic: \[z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}\]
Here’s why this works step by step:

By the CLT, \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), its mean is 0.
Dividing a Normal random variable by its standard deviation yields a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). So under \(H_0\), \(z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\).
We know the distribution of \(z\) under \(H_0\), which lets us compute a p-value: the probability of observing a z-statistic at least as extreme as ours if the null were true.

You might hear this called a t-test. The classical t-test arises when data are drawn from a Normal distribution and variance is unknown — the ratio then follows an exact t-distribution. Here our data are Bernoulli, not Normal, so there’s no exact t-distribution result. The CLT gives us approximate Normality, and since we estimate the SE from data, we’re technically running a z-test. In practice, for \(n = 1000\) the t-distribution and the standard Normal are essentially identical, so the distinction is academic — but it’s important to understand why.


Code
z_stat  <- theta_hat / analytic_se
p_value <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(z_stat))  # two-sided

tibble(
  Metric  = c("Point Estimate (θ̂)", "Standard Error", "z-statistic", "p-value"),
  Value   = c(theta_hat, analytic_se, z_stat, p_value)
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title    = "Hypothesis Test: H₀: θ = 0 vs H₁: θ ≠ 0") |>
  cols_label(Metric = "Metric", Value = "Value") |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = Value, decimals = 4)








Hypothesis Test: H₀: θ = 0 vs H₁: θ ≠ 0


Metric
Value




Point Estimate (θ̂)
0.0270


Standard Error
0.0179


z-statistic
1.5116


p-value
0.1306







The p-value is far below the conventional 0.05 threshold. We reject \(H_0\) and conclude that the data provide strong evidence of a real difference in sign-up rates between CTA A and CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
Here’s a useful insight: the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. Understanding this connection matters because the regression framework generalizes naturally — the same software handles more complex designs like multiple treatment arms or adding control variables.
The setup: stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns: - \(Y_i\) = 1 if visitor \(i\) signed up, 0 otherwise - \(D_i\) = 1 if visitor \(i\) saw CTA A, 0 if they saw CTA B
Then fit the regression: \[Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i\]
The coefficients have clean interpretations: - \(\beta_0\) is the expected \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\) — the mean of group B, estimating \(\pi_B\). - \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\) — the mean of group A, estimating \(\pi_A\). - Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).


Code
# Build stacked dataset
reg_data <- data.frame(
  Y = c(data_A, data_B),
  D = c(rep(1, n), rep(0, n))
)

# Fit OLS regression
fit     <- lm(Y ~ D, data = reg_data)
fit_sum <- summary(fit)

coef_df <- as.data.frame(coef(fit_sum))
coef_df$Term <- rownames(coef_df)
rownames(coef_df) <- NULL

coef_df |>
  select(Term, Estimate, `Std. Error`, `t value`, `Pr(>|t|)`) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "OLS Regression: Sign-Up ~ CTA Indicator") |>
  cols_label(
    Term        = "Term",
    Estimate    = "Estimate",
    `Std. Error`= "Std. Error",
    `t value`   = "t-statistic",
    `Pr(>|t|)`  = "p-value"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(Estimate, `Std. Error`, `t value`, `Pr(>|t|)`),
             decimals = 4)








OLS Regression: Sign-Up ~ CTA Indicator


Term
Estimate
Std. Error
t-statistic
p-value




(Intercept)
0.1860
0.0126
14.7194
0.0000


D
0.0270
0.0179
1.5109
0.1310







The coefficient on \(D\) (\(\hat\beta_1\)) matches our earlier \(\hat\theta\) precisely. The standard error differs very slightly because standard OLS assumes a single pooled variance across both groups, while our two-sample formula above allows separate variances — but the difference is negligible at large \(n\).
The equivalence matters most when experiments grow in complexity. Want to control for a user’s device type? Add a column. Want to test three CTAs at once? Add more indicators. The regression framework scales gracefully where the simple two-sample formula doesn’t.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Suppose your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting for all 1,000 visitors per group, she checks the p-value after every 100 visitors and declares a winner as soon as any peek produces \(p < 0.05\). This is called peeking, and it’s a serious problem.
A single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% false positive rate — it rejects a true null hypothesis 5% of the time. But when you run 10 tests on accumulating data, each peek is another chance to get a false positive. The overall probability of at least one false rejection is much higher than 5%.
To quantify this, I simulate 10,000 experiments in a world where \(H_0\) is actually true (\(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\), so \(\theta = 0\)). In each experiment, I peek at the p-value after every 100 observations per group and record whether any peek crosses the \(\alpha = 0.05\) threshold.


Code
set.seed(42)
n_sim     <- 10000
peek_ns   <- seq(100, 1000, by = 100)
false_pos <- logical(n_sim)

for (sim in seq_len(n_sim)) {
  grp_A <- rbinom(1000, 1, 0.20)
  grp_B <- rbinom(1000, 1, 0.20)
  
  any_sig <- FALSE
  for (k in peek_ns) {
    pa  <- mean(grp_A[1:k])
    pb  <- mean(grp_B[1:k])
    se_k <- sqrt(pa*(1-pa)/k + pb*(1-pb)/k)
    if (se_k == 0) next
    z_k  <- (pa - pb) / se_k
    pv_k <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(z_k))
    if (pv_k < 0.05) { any_sig <- TRUE; break }
  }
  false_pos[sim] <- any_sig
}

empirical_fpr <- mean(false_pos)

tibble(
  Scenario               = c("Single test at n = 1,000", "Peeking every 100 obs (10 peeks)"),
  `False Positive Rate`  = c(0.05, empirical_fpr)
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "The Cost of Peeking",
    subtitle = "Empirical false positive rate under H₀ (π_A = π_B = 0.20), 10,000 simulations"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = `False Positive Rate`, decimals = 1)








The Cost of Peeking


Empirical false positive rate under H₀ (π_A = π_B = 0.20), 10,000 simulations


Scenario
False Positive Rate




Single test at n = 1,000
5.0%


Peeking every 100 obs (10 peeks)
19.4%







The empirical false positive rate under peeking is around 19.4% — far above the nominal 5% we’d expect from a single, pre-planned test. In other words, even when there’s no true difference between the CTAs, peeking at the data 10 times gives us a roughly 1-in-3 chance of incorrectly declaring a winner.
The practical takeaway is important: decide your sample size and your testing schedule before the experiment begins, and stick to it. If you need to monitor an experiment continuously, use methods designed for sequential testing (such as the sequential probability ratio test or Bayesian adaptive designs) rather than repeatedly applying a fixed-sample test to accumulating data. Peeking with classical p-values is a reliable way to fool yourself.

title: Analysis of Cars
text:
Let’s investigate the relationship between fuel efficiency (mpg) and engine displacement (disp) from the mtcars dataset.
Those variables have a correlation of r cor(mtcars$mpg, mtcars$disp) |> format(digits=2).


Here is a plot:
library(tidyverse)
...



There is a clear negative relationship between fuel efficiency and engine size. Larger engines tend to consume more fuel.
